Using Adobe Express, they can combine video clips, images of their posters, voice-over narration, and text to produce a powerful call-to-action video to be shared during their visits to other Junior School classes.

For the 'Sort Out' activity, use a Padlet board to collaboratively map the researched NFPs. Create columns for different 'BIG ISSUES' (e.g., Animal Welfare, Health, Environment). Each research pair can post a summary of their NFP in the relevant column, allowing the whole class to visually identify common themes and form their action groups based on shared interests.

plannerText: This unit focuses on Not-for-Profit (NFP) organisations and their role in responding to community needs. Students research various NFPs in pairs, collating their findings in data charts to identify the 'big issues' each organisation addresses. Subsequently, students form action groups based on common interests, creating flyers and posters to promote and run fundraising or collection drives within the school community.

ci: Engineers design and build structures using specific features and materials to make them strong, stable and fit for purpose
lo: Different types of bridges and their design features; How materials and engineering principles contribute to structural strength and stability; Testing and refining designs through experimentation

s.d: Students will create a multi-page 'Bridge Engineer's Design Journal.' They can research and collate images and facts about famous beam, truss, and suspension bridges from around the world. They will then document their own design process, including initial sketches, photos of their construction, and a final report on their bridge's performance during weight testing, including an analysis of why it eventually failed.

In the design phase of the 'Building Bridge Challenge', students create a 3D digital prototype of their bridge in Tinkercad. This allows them to experiment with structural shapes like triangles and arches. They can then export images of their model into a PowerPoint slide, labelling the parts and writing a design justification that explains how their choices of features and materials will make the bridge strong and stable.

To document the 'Building Bridge Challenge', students use Stop Motion Studio on an iPad to create an animation of their construction process. They can also animate the testing phase, showing the incremental adding of coins until the bridge collapses. This visual record provides an excellent artefact for the 'Reflect and Act' stage, allowing students to analyse their bridge's failure point.

Connecting to the 'Tacoma Bridge Collapse' activity, students create a multimedia digital book in Book Creator to explain why some structures fail. They can combine text, images, and diagrams to explain the engineering flaws, embed a video of the collapse from Clickview, and record their own audio narration to present their findings on what makes a bridge unstable.

Students can use Tinkercad's 3D design space to digitally construct and prototype their bridge designs. They can apply their research on beam, truss, and suspension structures to create detailed models, testing ideas for stability and strength virtually before building their physical versions.

plannerText: This unit focuses on engineering principles where students investigate what makes structures strong, stable and fit for purpose. Key activities include researching different bridge designs like beam, truss, and suspension, analysing failures like the Tacoma Bridge Collapse, and then designing, building, and testing their own physical bridges to see how much weight they can hold before collapsing.

ci: First Nations people have a deep connection to Country, language and culture
lo: The diversity of language and culture across First Nations communities in Australia; The significance of connection to Country as custodial responsibility; Listening to and learning from Indigenous perspectives

s.d: To address the 'Sort Out' activity where students create a soundscape, they can use GarageBand on iPads to compose a multi-layered audio piece representing a specific First Nations Country. They can record sounds, use loops, and layer instruments to evoke the mood and animals from books like 'Welcome to Country'. App Smashing: Students then import their soundscape into a Microsoft PowerPoint slide, add a photo of their own acrylic painting (another unit task), and use the record narration feature to explain their musical and artistic choices and how they connect to their understanding of 'Country'.

Directly supporting the 'Find Out' task of mapping First Nations countries, students can use Google Earth to create an interactive map of Australia. They can place placemarks on the traditional lands of various language groups, and in the description box for each placemark, add researched information, images, and links to videos about that group's culture, language, or connection to the land, visually demonstrating the diversity of First Nations peoples.

Connecting to the assessment of retelling the Bunjil creation story, students can create a multimedia digital book. App Smashing: They first use the Brushes Redux app to create their own digital illustrations of key scenes, incorporating the First Nations symbols they have studied. They then import these artworks into Book Creator, add typed text for the narrative, and record their own voice reading the story, providing a rich demonstration of their understanding.

Students can create animated videos to retell the creation story of Bunjil, incorporating their own soundscapes of Country. They can also design digital artworks and infographics to map First Nations groups and showcase their understanding of traditional symbols, compiling their research and creations into a single digital portfolio.

To retell the Bunjil creation story, students choose an animated character to represent one of the animals from the narrative. They record a short segment of the story from that character's perspective, using their voice to convey the importance of the event. The clips can be compiled to create a class-wide animated retelling.

plannerText: This unit explores the central idea that First Nations people have a deep connection to Country, language, and culture. Key learning experiences include defining 'Country' from an Aboriginal perspective, mapping the diversity of First Nations groups, creating soundscapes representing the land, and retelling creation stories like that of Bunjil through various art forms and writing. The inquiry is supported by an excursion to the Melbourne Museum's First Nations exhibition and culminates in students creating personal artworks using traditional symbols.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration and discovery have led to changes that shape the world we live in
lo: The age of exploration and the reasons explorers set out on their journeys; How Australia changed through European arrival; Different perspectives on the consequences of exploration

ci: Leaders can help a group to function effectively
lo: Roles and responsibilities of effective leadership; Purpose of leadership; The strengths and qualities of a leader

ci: Social norms and stereotypes can influence the way people express themselves
lo: How stereotypes and social norms form and persist in society; The impact of discrimination, prejudice and assumptions on individuals and communities; How people overcome stereotypes to express themselves and inspire change

s.d: For the 'Go Further' research task on a famous person who overcame stereotypes, students can use the provided PowerPoint template to structure their research and gather key information. They can then 'App Smash' by exporting their completed slides as images and importing them into Book Creator. In Book Creator, students can add audio narration to explain their findings, embed relevant videos from Clickview, and create a more dynamic, interactive digital book to share with their peers.

Connecting to the 'Reflect and Act' phase and the student-initiated action of creating posters, students can use Canva to design a powerful digital poster or slogan challenging a specific stereotype. To extend this, they can import their Canva design into Adobe Express to create a short animated video. Students can add a voice-over, simple animations, and music to bring their message of expression and overcoming prejudice to life.

To support the 'Find Out' activities of defining terms and analysing stereotypes, create a class Padlet board. Use columns for 'What is a stereotype?', 'Examples in media', and 'How can we challenge them?'. Students can collaboratively post ideas, images, and links, turning the individual worksheet task into a dynamic and collective brainstorming session that captures the diverse perspectives in the room.

Building on their research from the 'Go Further' task, students use Adobe Express Podcasting to create a short series titled 'Expressions of Change'. Each group can script and record an episode about a famous person who challenged stereotypes, using the tool to edit their narrative, add theme music, and produce an authentic audio story.

To explore interpreting messages and body language, students choose a character in Adobe Express to represent a famous person or artwork. They record a short audio clip from the subject's perspective explaining what their expression or body language is communicating about social norms.

plannerText: This unit explores how social norms and stereotypes influence self-expression. Students begin by defining terms and analysing stereotypes in society through worksheets and scenarios. They then research a famous person who overcame prejudice, gathering information using a PowerPoint template to create an infographic or presentation. The unit encourages students to reflect on how public figures express themselves and to create their own posters and slogans to challenge stereotypes.

ci: Goods and services are exchanged within a marketplace
lo: The production of goods from raw materials through manufacturing to consumer; The exchange of currency for goods and services in a functioning economy; Ethics and responsible decision-making in the marketplace

s.d: Students will use Microsoft Teams as the central collaborative hub for the 'Launch Suburb' simulation. Each student business will have its own private channel to develop business proposals in Word, manage budgets in Excel, and plan their production chain. Teams will facilitate communication, file sharing, and project management, directly reflecting the unit's theme of organising complex economic systems.

For the 'Intro Spreadsheet/Budget' task, each student business group will use a pre-populated Microsoft Excel spreadsheet to manage their start-up bonus, track expenses, and calculate profit and loss during the trading sessions. To promote their businesses, they can use Canva to design professional logos, flyers, and price lists, which can then be inserted as images back into a 'Marketing' tab in their Excel workbook.

To demonstrate their understanding of 'Production Chains', students can create a multimedia digital book. Each page can detail a stage of their chosen product's journey: primary (raw materials), secondary (manufacturing), and final (retailing and distribution). Students can combine text, images, and audio narration to explain the process, showcasing a deep understanding of how goods are produced.

Following the 'Cause & Effect' activity where students watch BTN clips on inflation, they can use Adobe Express to create short, animated explainer videos. Students can animate simple characters and icons to illustrate an economic concept, such as how a change in supply affects the price of a popular good within their classroom marketplace, providing a creative way to articulate their understanding.

Before setting up their businesses, students can act as entrepreneurs and use Microsoft Forms to conduct market research. They can design a survey to poll their classmates (the consumers) on which goods and services are in highest demand. The automatically generated charts and data will help them make informed decisions when writing their business proposals, directly connecting to the unit's focus on supply and demand.

plannerText: Students explore economic concepts by defining financial terminology and investigating production chains, supply, and demand. The unit culminates in a large-scale simulation, 'Launch Suburb', where students design a city, apply for jobs, write business proposals, manage budgets with spreadsheets, and trade goods and services within their own classroom marketplace.

ci: Inventions and initiatives are a response to the changing needs of society
lo: How inventions have changed over time to meet the demands of society; The UN Sustainable Development Goals and the global problems they address; Using the design thinking process to create innovations that respond to real-world needs

s.d: For the 'Changed Over Time' research task, students can create an interactive timeline. They can design custom graphics in PowerPoint showing the evolution of their chosen invention, save them as images, and import these into a Book Creator e-book. Students can then add text and audio narration to explain the societal needs that drove each change, effectively 'app smashing' M365 with a creation tool to produce a rich, multimedia historical analysis.

Students will use Canva to document their entire design thinking journey. They can create infographics to present their research on a chosen Sustainable Development Goal, design digital blueprints and mockups of their prototype invention, and produce a professional-quality presentation to pitch their innovative idea at the 'Future Thinkers' Forum'.

For the 'Prototype' phase, student groups whose inventions involve mechanical or robotic elements will build a functional proof-of-concept using Lego Spike Prime. They will code it to perform a key function related to their SDG solution. To present their prototype at the Future Thinkers' Forum, they will film it in action and edit the footage in iMovie, adding titles and a voiceover pitch to create a compelling demonstration video.

To support the 'SDGs further investigation' task, after students select their top 3 SDGs using Microsoft Forms as planned, they can use a collaborative Padlet board. Each group can use a column for their chosen SDG to collate initial research, posting links, images, and key facts from the provided infographics. This creates a shared visual space for brainstorming before moving into the formal design process in their PowerPoints.

To create a dynamic presentation for the Future Thinkers' Forum, students can film a 'news report' about their invention using a green screen. They can superimpose themselves onto a background that illustrates the problem their invention solves. They can then edit this footage in Adobe Express, adding text overlays, graphics, and a call to action to create a persuasive and professional video pitch for their initiative.

plannerText: Students investigate past and present inventions, exploring how they have evolved over time to meet the changing needs of society. The unit culminates in a 'Future Thinkers' Forum' where students, following a design thinking process, research a Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) and create their own prototype invention or initiative to address a global problem.

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways
lo: How community values shape the management of the natural environment; The need for conservation and environmental stewardship; Ways in which First Nations land management practices can drive positive environmental change

s.d: Students will use a dedicated Team as a central hub for the 'Sharing the Planet' unit. Within the integrated Class Notebook, they will collaboratively collate research on world biomes, habitats, and Indigenous land management perspectives. Channels can be created for groups to share findings from their virtual explorations using Google Earth, plan their native garden bed designs, and work together on their final advocacy posters.

Students will use Canva to design digital advocacy posters about conservation and stewardship. They can create annotated visual maps of world biomes and develop a 2D, top-down blueprint for their proposed native garden beds, incorporating research on local flora and Indigenous land management principles.

Following their visit to Chum Creek and ECOSS, students can create a multimedia reflection journal in Book Creator. They can import photos and videos taken on the excursion using the iPad's camera, add text boxes to document what they learned (mirroring the written reflections in the planner), and record audio narration to explain the importance of the conservation efforts they witnessed. This creates a rich, multimodal recount of their off-campus learning.

Directly supporting the student-initiated action of designing garden beds, students can use the Micro:bit [Helping Plants Grow](https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=helping-plants-grow) lesson to build and code a soil moisture sensor. This project provides a technological solution for conserving water in their garden, demonstrating a positive interaction between communities and the environment.

For the student-initiated action of designing garden beds, students can build a working prototype of a smart-watering system using Lego Spike Prime. They can use sensors to detect low moisture levels and program a motor to simulate a water pump, providing a hands-on engineering solution to a real-world environmental challenge.

plannerText: This unit explores conservation and stewardship, with students investigating different world environments, habitats, and Indigenous land management perspectives. Key learning experiences include a gallery walk, vocabulary building, visiting the ECOSS environmental centre, and a unique activity where students write advocacy emails to trees on the Melbourne campus. Students demonstrate their understanding by creating posters, mapping biomes, and designing potential native garden beds for the school.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Colonisation has had a lasting impact on different countries and their people
lo: The reasons, methods and impact of colonisation; How colonisation transformed society and culture; The responsibility of colonisers and the perspectives of those colonised

s.d: To deepen their analysis of 'The Rabbits' from the 'Tune In' phase, students can create a collaborative digital book. Using PowerPoint, they can design custom graphics like the 'sunglasses' template mentioned in the planner to represent different perspectives. They can then import these images into Book Creator, adding audio narration to explain their thinking and embedding the Australian History video from the 'Find Out' phase to create a rich, multimedia analysis of colonisation's impact.

During the 'Find Out' research phase, students can use Google Earth's creation tools to map the global reach of the British Empire or the specific voyages of colonisers to Australia. They can add placemarks with historical details and images at key locations. Students can then screen record a narrated virtual tour of their map, explaining the process and impact of colonisation, and upload the video to their Seesaw journal to share their findings.

Students will use Canva to synthesise their research on colonisation. They can create compelling visual aids such as timelines, infographics, and maps for their final oral presentation on their chosen country. Canva can also be used to visually deconstruct the themes and perspectives in the picture book 'The Rabbits'.

Students can create a short 'Point of View' video about the arrival of colonisers using Adobe Express. They can choose to represent either the coloniser or an Indigenous person, combining archival images, text, and their own voice-over narration to create a powerful video that explores the vastly different perspectives on the same historical event.

For the research project on Britain, set up collaborative channels in Microsoft Teams for student groups. Each group can use an embedded Padlet board as a digital wall to collate their research, posting links, key facts from the ClickView videos, images, and questions. This creates a collaborative and visual workspace for students to organise their information before creating their final presentations, allowing the teacher to monitor progress in real-time.

plannerText: This unit explores the impact of colonisation, beginning with an analysis of the picture book 'The Rabbits' to understand different perspectives. Students then conduct research into the history of British colonisation in Australia using videos and online resources. The unit culminates in students choosing a country, researching its colonisation, and delivering an oral presentation of their findings to the class.

ci: Values, beliefs and actions contribute to a diverse society
lo: How our values and beliefs shape our identity and interactions; Our responsibility to manage emotions and be safe online and in person; The importance of resilience, respect and positive relationships in a diverse community

s.d: To address the 'CyberSafety' lessons and the student inquiry about being an 'upstander', students can create a Public Service Announcement (PSA) video. They can first use PowerPoint to storyboard their message, creating slides with key text and images. These slides can then be imported into Adobe Express, where students add voice narration, animation, and music to produce a compelling video on responsible digital citizenship.

Following the 'UR Strong' and 'CyberSafety' sessions, students can collaboratively create a digital 'Guide to Wellbeing'. Using Canva, each group can design an infographic or comic strip illustrating a key concept, such as their 'golden rule' for online behaviour or how to handle a 'friendship fire'. These assets can then be imported into a shared Book Creator book, where students add text and audio reflections to explain their understanding.

To assess understanding of the difference between social misunderstandings and hurtful behaviour, create a Microsoft Form with various real-world scenarios. Students will read each scenario from the 'UR Strong' lessons and choose the best way to respond as a resilient and responsible friend. Branching logic can be used to provide immediate, targeted feedback based on their answers, reinforcing the unit's key concepts.

As a digital extension of the 't-shirt about me' Tune In activity, create a class Padlet wall. Students can post text, images, or drawings that represent their personal values and how they connect to the IB Learner Profile. This activity directly supports the unit's focus on identity and making connections between personal beliefs and community values, creating a visual representation of the diverse class community.

Students will use Canva's design tools to create their personal 't-shirt about me', visually representing their identity and values as explored in the Grow Your Mind program. They can also design impactful posters or infographics illustrating their 'golden rule' for cybersafety, promoting positive and safe online relationships. These creations can be compiled into a multi-page Canva design, serving as a digital portfolio of their learning.

plannerText: This unit focuses on wellbeing, identity, and relationships, particularly in online contexts for students settling into Middle School. Students engage in explicit programs like CyberSafety, URSTRONG, and Grow Your Mind to learn about managing emotions, online safety, resilience, and positive social interactions. Key activities include creating a personal 't-shirt about me', developing a 'golden rule' for cybersafety, and reflecting on their learning through portfolio tasks and Seesaw activities.

ci: Understanding global issues and using effective communication empowers us to raise awareness and inspire action.
lo: Global issues are caused by interconnected factors that require collective solutions (Causation) ??? Presentations in the media can reflect the perspective of the author rather than the truth (Perspective) ??? We must make responsible choices when responding to and creating media (Responsibility) ??? Messages can be expressed through a range of mediums and elements can be adjusted to enhance effectiveness (Communication) ??? Design choices contribute to the effectiveness of a message (Influence)

ci: The way a country is governed impacts the lives of citizens.
lo: The key institutions and functions of Australia's democratic system of government (Function) ??? How government decisions impact citizens at federal, state and local levels (Causation) ??? The rights and responsibilities of Australian citizens and their obligations as global citizens (Equality/Citizenship)

s.d: For the 'Local Government Task' in the Go Further section, students will research their local council. They can use Canva to design visually engaging infographics about council services, a custom logo, and a map of their LGA. These assets can then be imported into a Microsoft PowerPoint presentation to structure their research on the council's name, services, and their family's personal connections to them, combining Canva's design flair with PowerPoint's presentation capabilities.

To demonstrate their understanding of 'Passing a Bill in Parliament', student groups can design and build an automated 'parliamentary voting' machine using Lego Spike Prime. They can code it to tally 'Aye' and 'No' votes from colour sensors and display the result. They can share a video of their machine in action on Teams to explain the process.

Connecting to the '3 Levels of Government' and Federation map activities, students can use Google Earth to locate and place markers on their local council chambers, state parliament, and federal parliament in Canberra. They can take screenshots of these annotated locations and import them into Book Creator to produce a multi-page digital book explaining the distinct roles and responsibilities of each level of government, creating an interactive virtual tour.

For the 'Bill to Parliament' task, student groups can create a persuasive 'call to action' video using Adobe Express. They can script their argument for the bill, combine video of themselves debating, text overlays with key statistics, and background music to produce a compelling media piece designed to sway the vote of their parliamentary peers.

For the 'Bill to Parliament' task in the Reflect and Act section, students can post a summary of their proposed bill on a class Padlet wall to simulate a parliamentary debate, where peers can post arguments for and against. To conclude the debate, the student group will create a Microsoft Form to serve as an official ballot, allowing the class to vote 'Yes' or 'No' on the bill and provide a justification, mirroring a democratic voting process.

plannerText: This unit explores the function of government and its impact on citizens' lives. Students investigate the three levels of Australian government, the process of passing a bill in parliament, and the rights and responsibilities of citizenship. Key activities include researching local councils to create a PowerPoint, planning their own bill to propose, and reflecting on their educational trip to Canberra.

ci: The relationships between the Earth, Moon and Sun create predictable patterns that shape life on our planet and are understood through both scientific and cultural perspectives.
lo: The movement of the Earth, Moon and Sun causes predictable patterns such as day/night, moon phases, tides and seasons (Causation) ??? Different cultures, including Indigenous knowledge systems, interpret the night sky in unique and meaningful ways (Perspective) ??? The Earth, Moon and Sun have specific structures, positions and relationships that can be represented through models and simulations (Form)

s.d: To animate the lunar cycle for Lesson 5, students can use iPads and Stop Motion Studio. They can photograph a model (using a ball and light source, or even Oreo cookies as suggested in the planner) at each stage, creating a film that visually explains the moon phases. App smash by adding explanatory voiceovers in iMovie and sharing the final video in a class Microsoft Teams channel for peer feedback.

To create a dynamic model of day and night for Lesson 3, students can program a Sphero BOLT to both rotate on its axis and orbit a central light source (the 'Sun'). They can use the BOLT's LED matrix to visually represent the illuminated and dark sides of the Earth. Students can then screen record their working model and use Explain Everything to annotate the video, demonstrating their understanding of Earth's rotation.

Connecting to Lesson 7's focus on cultural perspectives, students can research an Indigenous Australian constellation and its associated story. They can then use Book Creator to produce a multimedia digital book, app smashing with Canva to create custom illustrations of the constellation. Students can record themselves narrating the story and embed the audio, sharing their final book via a link in the class OneNote.

Directly supporting the 'Go Further' podcast task, students use Adobe Express Podcasting to script and record an episode on a chosen space topic (e.g., tides, seasons). They can leverage the tool's easy-to-use editing and AI-powered audio enhancement to create a polished, informative episode for the class 'SpaceCast' series.

Students can create animated explainer videos to model and narrate the Earth's rotation causing day and night, the Moon's orbit causing its phases, or the Earth's tilted axis resulting in the seasons. They can also design infographics on Indigenous astronomical knowledge or create compelling cover art and audio grams to promote their final research podcast.

plannerText: This unit explores the relationship between the Earth, Moon, and Sun, with students investigating concepts like day and night, seasons, moon phases, and tides through hands-on activities like using globes, flashlights, and Oreo cookies. The inquiry also examines different cultural perspectives on astronomy, including Indigenous knowledge, and culminates in students creating a research podcast on a chosen topic.

ci: Peoples' actions affect the balance of nature.
lo: What the environment is comprised of and how ecosystems function (Form) ??? Our responsibility to care for and protect the natural world (Responsibility) ??? The interdependence between humans, animals and the environment (Interdependence) ??? The consequences of human actions on the balance of nature (Consequences)

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Human migration is a response to challenges, risks and opportunities.
lo: The causes for and consequences of migration (Causation) ??? The changes in societies as a result of migration (Change) ??? The impact of migration on the individual, both positive and negative (Impact) ??? Migration results in a more diverse society (Diversity)

ci: Understanding ourselves, our relationships and our wellbeing empowers us to thrive.
lo: The recognisable features of safe and healthy interactions (Form) ??? The connections between emotions, behaviour, learning, interactions and goals (Connection) ??? The tools and skills to stay safe online and in person (Safety) ??? The active role we play in achieving and maintaining our wellbeing (Wellbeing) ??? The strategies to achieve and maintain positive relationships (Relationships)

s.d: Connecting to the 'Listen to Podcast' activity, students use Adobe Express Podcasting to script and record their own short wellbeing podcasts. In groups, they can discuss a chosen wellbeing strategy and use the platform's simple editing tools to create a supportive and informative episode to share with peers via Microsoft Sway.

To build on the 'ROAR wellbeing poster' and 'Essential Agreement' activities, students can use Canva to co-design a visually engaging digital poster or infographic representing their class's shared values. To facilitate ongoing reflection, a QR code linking to a Microsoft Forms survey can be added to the design, allowing students to anonymously provide feedback on how the agreement is working or share wellbeing successes.

To extend the 'Portrait Drawing Task', students can create a digital 'All About Me' book using Book Creator. They can use the iPad's Digital Camera to import their physical drawing and then add multimedia elements on other pages, such as video clips explaining their hobbies, audio recordings reflecting on their strengths, and text detailing how they embody their chosen IB Learner Profiles.

Microsoft Teams will serve as the central hub for this unit. Students can use the Posts tab for collaborative discussions on resilience and inclusion, sharing their personal artworks based on the ROAR poster and IB Learner Profiles. The integrated Class Notebook provides each student with a private digital journal to document their self-understanding and set personal wellbeing and executive functioning goals, effectively replacing both Padlet and OneNote within a single, secure platform.

Students can use Adobe Express to design compelling digital artworks that visually represent their personal connection to the ROAR wellbeing poster and the IB Learner Profiles. They can also create short, reflective videos to articulate their understanding of resilience and inclusion, or to set and share their personal executive functioning goals in a dynamic, multimedia format, truly capturing 'Who We Are'.

plannerText: This unit focuses on student wellbeing, relationships, and self-understanding, with a central idea that this knowledge empowers them to thrive. Key activities include creating personal artworks based on the ROAR wellbeing poster and IB Learner Profiles, engaging with podcasts and discussions on resilience and inclusion, and setting personal goals related to executive functioning skills.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time

s.d: To support the oral storytelling activity in Week 1, students can take a photo of their baby picture or a drawing of themselves. They can then use ChatterPix Kids to draw a mouth and record themselves telling a short story about 'When I was little...', bringing their past self to life.

Directly connecting to the 'My Life in 3 Parts' sequencing activity in Week 4, students can create a simple 3-page digital book. They can import photos or use the built-in drawing tools for each stage ('baby', 'little', 'now') and use the audio record button to narrate each page, creating a personal multimodal timeline.

For the 'My special object tells a story' lesson in Week 3, students can use the Seesaw app on their iPads. They can take a photo of their special artefact and use the record tool to orally explain its story and why it is important to them, creating a digital class museum.

To enhance the Week 2 activity of sorting old vs new objects, create a class Padlet board with two columns labelled with images for 'Old' and 'New'. Students can use iPads to take photos of objects around the school or from a collection in class and upload them to the correct column, creating a collaborative, visual sort.

To explore the concept of change over time, students can work in small groups to create a short animation showing a simple sequence, like a person growing from a baby to a child using drawings or play-doh. This reinforces the sequencing skills from Week 4 in a creative, hands-on format. The final video can be shared on Seesaw for peer feedback.

plannerText: This unit explores personal history and the concept of past and present through the central idea 'Discovering our past helps us know more about ourselves today'. Students engage in activities such as sharing baby photos and family artefacts, creating simple 'baby to now' timelines, and using oral storytelling to share memories and personal stories. The inquiry focuses on sequencing events and understanding how objects can tell us about the past.
